results.response_text:
Several individuals named Evan Huang work as software engineers or in related technology fields, each with distinct specializations and projects:

One Evan Huang is a software engineer and solo developer focused on building "living systems" and interfaces. He is currently pursuing an MS in Computer Science at New York University (2023–2025), having previously earned a BA in Computer Science & Economics from the same institution (2019–2023). His projects include "World Engine," an LLM-driven world for autonomous agents, "Agilee" for real-time agile meeting collaboration, and "Renter-X" for NYC rentals. He has experience with technologies such as Django, Flask, SQL, Python, and MongoDB.

Another Evan Huang identifies as a former Frontend Developer, currently prioritizing his studies but maintaining a passion for coding and problem-solving. He possesses strong experience with React, Next.js, and TypeScript. He founded Tech-Art Studio, a community-driven web development studio, and his featured projects include "One Calendar," a privacy-first calendar web app, and a password-protected email sending application called "Mail."

A third Evan Huang, found on GitHub under the username "evanqhuang," focuses on distributed systems, developer tooling, and occasionally underwater hardware. His projects include "HostelHawk," a price-history tracker for Hostelworld, and "Camazotz," which involves designing and selling cave diving equipment, including an underwater cave-mapping device. He utilizes technologies such as SvelteKit, Rust, TimescaleDB, Fusion, and C.

There is also an Evan Huang who is a Full Stack Data Scientist at Quantifind since October 2022, having previously worked as a Data Scientist at the same company. He holds a Master's Degree in Electrical Engineering from Stanford University (2017–2019), where he researched optimizing objective formulas for GAN architecture. He also earned a Bachelor of Science and Engineering and a Master's Degree in Systems Engineering from the University of Pennsylvania (2013–2017). His professional experience includes roles as a Research Intern at AQR Capital Management and an I.T. Portfolio Coordinator Intern at Keurig Green Mountain, Inc.

I'll search for information about Evan Huang and his projects.
Based on the search results, there appear to be multiple people named Evan Huang. The most detailed information comes from the software engineer Evan Huang. Here are his specific projects:

## Embedded Systems & Hardware


An underwater cave survey system with custom hardware, bare-metal firmware, and a post-dive 3D analysis tool. The handheld device tracks a diver's position as they swim through a cave and renders a live map on a touchscreen display, with logged data loading into a web visualizer that reconstructs the cave in 3D.



A 90% 3D-printed toolchanger for FDM printers that achieves sub-millimeter repeatability with no sensors, no solenoids, and no electronics on the dock. The coupling geometry constrains tool position through contact alone, with nine neodymium magnets providing clamping force.


## Web & Real Estate


A platform that automates commercial real estate due diligence — searching listings, analyzing properties, and comparing pricing across multiple data sources through a natural-language chat interface. It was built across four services in three languages, coordinated by a shared protocol layer, and deployed on GCP with Terraform.


## Data & Analytics


A Rust HTTP API over a unified flights reference database with 3.3M flights, 38k routes, and 8.5k airports, built by merging OpenFlights and the historical FlightDB schedule archive. It was deployed to Oracle Cloud Free Tier with all ingress running through a Cloudflare Tunnel.



A project implementing an alternative physics model for scuba decompression that models gas absorption as spatial diffusion, comparing this against the dominant industry model across approximately 7,800 dive profiles.


## E-commerce


An e-commerce site for a cave diving gear company where every product is designed from scratch in CAD and 3D-printed in ASA plastic, with customers able to configure colors on an interactive 3D model before checkout.

There are several individuals named Evan Huang who have built various projects across different fields. Here are some of their notable contributions:

**Evan Huang (Inventor of DrinkBot):**
*   **DrinkBot:** A robot designed to grab and deliver cups for the elderly and disabled, aiming to improve their quality of life. He created a second prototype with obstacle-detecting sensors.
*   **Rise NZ Club:** Founded in 2022 to encourage and support young people in New Zealand to create their own "Rise projects" and contribute to the community.
*   **VEX Robotics Challenge:** Represented New Zealand at the VEX Robotics World Championship, placing 4th in his division.

**Evan Huang (Software Developer/Hardware Engineer - evanqhuang on GitHub):**
*   **HostelHawk:** A price-history tracker for Hostelworld inventory built with SvelteKit, Rust, and TimescaleDB.
*   **Camazotz:** Involves designing and selling cave diving equipment, including a handheld underwater cave-mapping device with real-time navigation.
*   **FinFeed:** A live fish-tank camera streamed to any browser over HLS, utilizing Raspberry Pi, FFmpeg, and hls.js.
*   **geo-daemon:** A weekly tracker for brand visibility across AI search engines, built with TypeScript.
*   **maxwells-razor:** A simpler, modular, and largely 3D-printed 3D printer toolchanger.
*   **camazotz-ixchel:** RP2350 firmware for a handheld underwater cave mapping device.
*   **dbm3:** A slab diffusion decompression model backtested against Buhlmann ZH-L16C.
*   **T480s-hackintosh:** A Clover EFI folder for a T480s running Catalina 10.15.7.

**Evan Huang (Product Designer):**
*   **Unmanned Golf Booking Platform.**
*   **Funeral Flower Ordering Automation Platform.**
*   **Connected:** A social media side project.
*   **One Gobo.**
*   **One Gobo CRM.**
*   **Resend email sending web application:** Built with NextJS and password protected.

**Evan Huang (Real Estate, Business Development, and Investment):**
*   **Baohaus:** Co-founded the critically acclaimed restaurant.
*   **Velocity Capital Group:** Co-founder and managing director of this private-equity investment firm, focusing on multi-family investments in high-growth American markets.
*   **Batu Capital:** As Managing Partner, he leads investments in real estate, consumer goods, health, and fintech. This firm has also been noted for investing in cannabis, crypto, and big data startups.
*   **Titan Capital Management:** Served as Managing Partner, specializing in cross-border investments.

It is important to note that the search results also included a fictional character named Evan Huang from the show "Fresh Off the Boat," who, in one instance, built a contraption to break down Dr. Pepper into its 23 flavors for a science fair.

results.response_text:
Evan Huang, whose full name is Sheng-En Huang, is involved in several collaborative research projects focused on autonomous underwater cave exploration and mapping. These initiatives aim to develop artificial intelligence and robotic solutions to enable Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) to navigate and map complex underwater cave systems. He collaborates with researchers such as Dr. Ioannis Rekleitis and Dr. Ramtin Zand.

Key aspects of the underwater cave mapping projects Evan Huang is involved with include:

*   **Caveline Detection at the Edge** The project focuses on developing lightweight caveline detection models for deployment on edge platforms like Raspberry Pi-4, Intel Neural Compute Stick, and Nvidia Jetson Nano. The objective is to enable underwater robots to identify and follow these permanent guide ropes, which are crucial for navigation within caves. Detecting and following cavelines is paramount for autonomous exploration and mapping missions.
*   **Semantic Segmentation and Scene Parsing (CaveSeg Project)** This project introduces CaveSeg, a visual learning pipeline for semantic segmentation and scene parsing tailored for AUV navigation inside underwater caves. It addresses the challenge of limited annotated data by creating a comprehensive dataset with pixel annotations for important navigation markers (like cavelines and arrows), obstacles (such as ground planes and overhead layers), scuba divers, and open areas for maneuvering. The project utilizes a computationally light, novel transformer-based model that offers near real-time execution and state-of-the-art performance.
*   **Vision Transformer (ViT)-based Learning Pipeline (CL-ViT Project)** In the CL-ViT project, a novel Vision Transformer (ViT)-based learning pipeline is presented for fast caveline detection in images from underwater caves. This approach uses a weakly supervised method, allowing for rapid model adaptation to data from new locations with minimal ground truth labels. The CL-ViT model balances robustness and efficiency, ensuring good generalization performance.

The overarching goal of these projects is to assist human divers by autonomously collecting water quality data, mapping topography, and navigating dangerous cave regions. This ultimately helps in exploring uncharted areas, mapping unmapped sections, and uncovering archaeological mysteries within these vital freshwater resources. The research is supported in part by the National Science Foundation.

queries.prompt: What can you tell me about evanqhuang.com?

results.response_text:
Evan Huang is a software engineer with experience in both embedded systems and full-stack web development. He is currently employed at Capital One in McLean, VA, where he has contributed to various projects, including:

- Architecting a high-throughput, event-driven card transaction processing system using Go, Restate, Lambda, and Kafka, achieving a 15-minute Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) through idempotent processing and active-active design.

- Developing pre-approval and eligibility flows for a small-business credit card platform, serving millions of applicants and contributing an annualized value of $60 million with an 18% conversion lift.

- Creating a full-stack team capacity dashboard using Svelte 5, Go, and AWS CDK/Lambda/S3, delivering the project solo in four days, significantly ahead of the estimated six to eight weeks.

- Identifying and resolving a schema-versioning defect in the organization's event-streaming platform, leading to the development of Recadero—a Go CLI with auto-resolution, schema validation, and pre-commit hooks—adopted across teams and driving the legacy library to end-of-life organization-wide.

- Leading a cross-team initiative on AI-assisted development by building a multi-agent rubric evaluation framework assessing 13 internal tools and shipping a suite of AI-augmented development tools, including automated code review and Jira automation, recovering approximately 140 engineering hours per year.

- Addressing a bottleneck in the Elm functional test suite, reducing local and CI/CD time by 50% (from 400 seconds to 200 seconds) and saving over 150 engineering hours per month across more than 30 engineers.

- Developing a parallel Python orchestration system for new account booking, coordinating transactions across more than five microservices with event-driven Kafka validation, enabling a migration of over 140 million accounts.

- Building a knowledge-graph platform using Neo4j and FastAPI that ingests codebases via tree-sitter Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) parsing and AI vector embeddings for semantic search, linking product specifications to implementation code across repositories.

- Independently solving a developer experience bottleneck affecting over 30 engineers by building a recursive-descent parser in Python to decode cryptic Elm/Morphir compiler errors, reducing diagnosis time from 10 minutes to under one minute.

- Designing production monitoring from scratch, including structured logging with Splunk, PagerDuty escalation policies, and Service Level Agreement (SLA) alerting, achieving a zero-downtime launch with full observability into distributed system health.

- Developing a shared Go SDK for Restate durable-workflow services adopted across teams, standardizing handler patterns, testing infrastructure, and idempotency guarantees.

In addition to his professional experience, Evan has worked on several personal projects:

- **Ixchel**: A complete underwater cave survey system that includes custom hardware, bare-metal firmware, and a post-dive 3D analysis tool. The handheld device tracks a diver's position as they swim through a cave and renders a live map on a touchscreen display. Logged data loads into a web visualizer that reconstructs the cave in 3D.

- **Maxwell's Razor**: A 90% 3D-printed toolchanger for Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) printers that achieves sub-millimeter repeatability without sensors, solenoids, or electronics on the dock. The coupling geometry constrains tool position through contact alone, with nine neodymium magnets providing clamping force. Designed for the Hypercube EVO with full Klipper macro automation.

- **Camazotz Diving**: An e-commerce site for a cave diving gear company Evan started, where every product is designed from scratch in CAD and 3D-printed in ASA plastic. Customers can configure and purchase specialized diving equipment directly from the website.

Evan holds a Bachelor of Science in Computer Science from The Ohio State University, where he was also the Vice President of the ACM Student Chapter. His technical skills include Go, Python, Kafka, Restate, AWS, React, and more. For a comprehensive view of his work and experience, you can visit his personal website at [evanqhuang.com](https://evanqhuang.com).

Several organizations and individuals are developing hardware and software solutions for underwater cave mapping, catering to both professional and recreational divers. Here are some notable examples:


[Mapping underwater terrain with bathymetric LiDAR | Leica Geosystems](https://leica-geosystems.com/case-studies/natural-resources/mapping-underwater-terrain-with-bathymetric-lidar?utm_source=openai)
**Depthline**  
Depthline offers sonar-powered cave mapping tools designed for recreational divers. Their system provides real-time 3D mapping of underwater cave systems, enhancing visibility and safety during dives. ([depth-line.com](https://depth-line.com/?utm_source=openai))



[MNemo | Cave survey data acquisition | Ariane's Line](https://www.arianesline.com/mnemo/?utm_source=openai)
**MNemo by Ariane's Line**  
MNemo is a cave survey tool that records standard cave survey measurements, including distance, azimuth, and inclination, in a single 3-second reading. It integrates with Ariane's 3D cave mapping software, facilitating efficient and accurate cave mapping. ([arianesline.com](https://www.arianesline.com/mnemo/?utm_source=openai))



[Home](https://caveatron.com/?utm_source=openai)
**Caveatron**  
Caveatron is a compact, handheld electronic device designed for mapping caves and other underground environments. It functions as a cave survey tool and, with its integrated LIDAR, captures full 3D scans of the cave interior. ([caveatron.com](https://caveatron.com/?utm_source=openai))



[Cerulean Omniscan 450 FS イメージングソナー - 水中ドローンとUSV（水上ドローン）の開発・修理・販売｜全国対応の株式会社チック](https://www.rov-fun.com/cerulean-omniscan-450-fs-imaging-sonar/?utm_source=openai)
**Omniscan 3D 450 SS Modular by Cerulean Sonar**  
The Omniscan 3D 450 SS Modular is an advanced underwater imaging system that bridges the gap between side-scan sonar and multibeam echo sounders. It generates high-resolution 3D point clouds in real time, suitable for inspection, mapping, and navigation. ([ceruleansonar.com](https://ceruleansonar.com/product/omniscan-3d/?utm_source=openai))



[DiveMate | Innovation for Divers](https://www.divemate.de/?utm_source=openai)
**CaveDiveMap App**  
CaveDiveMap is an iPhone app designed for cave diving survey and automated mapping. It requires a 3D-printed measuring wheel attached to a waterproof dive case for the iPhone, enabling quick, automated one-handed surveying of sumps and underwater caves. ([apps.apple.com](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/cavedivemap/id6743342160?utm_source=openai))


These solutions range from compact handheld devices to advanced sonar systems, catering to various needs in underwater cave exploration and mapping.

results.response_text:
I'll search for information about who is building DIY underwater cave mapping hardware.
Based on the search results, there are several individuals and groups building DIY underwater cave mapping hardware:

## Individual Enthusiasts


One developer has been working on mapping caves using visual-inertial SLAM, with a goal to use VI-SLAM to map out accurate underwater trajectories and add an instrument tube to the front of a DPV that will house a 360-degree blue-light LiDAR to get accurate cave cross-sections.
 
The hardware so far costs approximately $1,000 and can be carried by hand or attached to a DPV.
 
They plan to make the data and derivatives available for free under a strong copyleft license.


Another builder has created 
a "poor man's mnemo survey/mapping device" that uses an iPhone's internal sensors to survey a cave with reasonable accuracy.
 
The iOS app is free and open source, as well as the STL and Fusion 360 designs.


## Research Projects

There's also 
the 3D-CAVE research team, which incorporates senior investigators and specialists with backgrounds in computer architectures, application-specific accelerators, 3D reconstruction, Visual SLAM, and Artificial Intelligence.


These DIY and research-driven approaches aim to make underwater cave mapping more accessible and affordable compared to existing commercial solutions.

results.response_text:
A growing community of individuals, researchers, and open-source enthusiasts are actively building DIY underwater cave mapping hardware. These efforts often leverage readily available and low-cost components, alongside open-source software, to create innovative solutions for exploring and mapping submerged cave systems.

Notable examples and projects include:

*   **Individual Makers and Enthusiasts:**
    *   One project details a "Cave Mapper" built around a Raspberry Pi Pico 2, incorporating sensors like a magnetic encoder for distance, an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) for orientation, and a pressure sensor for depth. This device aims to allow divers to create real-time stick maps during exploration.
    *   Another individual, identified as "9nl," developed a DIY sensor system for 3D cave mapping. This system utilizes a spinning 2D lidar sensor assembly and a Raspberry Pi 5 to generate three-dimensional maps.
    *   Addison Snyder is also working on a self-contained LiDAR setup specifically designed for use in underwater caves.
    *   Early DIY efforts also include devices assembled for underwater cave surveying using components like the Adafruit BN0055 '9 DoF IMU' within a waterproof housing to improve accuracy over traditional methods.
*   **Researchers and Collaborative Projects:**
    *   Richard Wylde and his colleagues are involved in mapping subterranean cenotes in Yucatan, Mexico. Their work incorporates a Raspberry Shake, a Raspberry Pi-based device typically used for detecting earthquakes, for acoustic and magnetic mapping of these cave systems.
    *   Tim Hansen and Andreas Birk from Constructor University have presented an open-source software framework that enables real-time underwater mapping. This framework is designed to work with affordable marine robotics components such as the BlueRobotics BlueROV2 and its Ping360 Mechanical Scanning Sonar.
    *   The "Awesome-Hydrospatial" GitHub repository serves as a curated list of publicly available resources for the ocean mapping community, showcasing various open-source tools and projects developed by institutions, researchers, and enthusiasts. These include software like OpenSideScan for sidescan sonar imagery and Kluster for hydrographic processing, as well as hardware projects like OpenCTD, a low-cost, open-source CTD (Conductivity, Temperature, Depth) device.
    *   There are also efforts in the broader marine mapping community, such as the DIY glider design by Alex Williams, which focuses on collecting and analyzing underwater images.

results.response_text:
Building DIY underwater cave mapping hardware is a niche but active field, driven primarily by speleologists, tech-divers, and roboticists seeking to bypass the high costs of commercial mapping gear.
The most notable open-source and DIY builders include:
Addison Snyder (Free Springs LLC): Operating out of Gainesville, Florida, Snyder has been actively building and prototyping self-contained, custom LiDAR and SLAM mapping setups specifically for use in underwater caves.
The CaveDiveMapping Community:
This is an open-source movement that has gained consensus among diving enthusiasts. They have built an affordable cave survey system that pairs a standard smartphone (like an iPhone) running free software with a 3D-printed measuring wheel. The wheel uses a small magnet to trigger the phone's magnetic field sensor, tracking distance traveled through the flooded passages.
Sebastien Kister (Ariane’s Line): While starting as an electronic survey tool, Kister’s device inspired numerous DIY variants. It uses a small box clipped to the cave guideline with a mechanical wheel that measures distance, compass heading, and depth.
Dr. Janusz Będkowski and Michał Pełka: These developers created Mandeye, an affordable, open-source 3.5D LiDAR and surveying system primarily designed for dry caves but actively modified by the speleology and surveying communities for related underground mapping tasks
.
Academic & Hobbyist Robotics (e.g., Sunfish/Stone Aerospace): While originally commercial and academic projects (such as the DEPTHX and Sunfish autonomous underwater vehicles), the open-source and DIY robotic communities frequently reference and attempt to replicate their sonar-based Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) hardware. Blue Robotics +9
In addition to custom hardware, many DIY cave mappers rely on open-source processing programs like Cavewhere
or Ariane's Line to render their data into accurate 3D maps. Ariane's Line +2

The Camazotz Cave Mapping Project is an initiative by Camazotz Cohaven, a community-driven hospitality and co-living space located in the Yucatán Peninsula, Mexico. This project involves the exploration and mapping of a small limestone cave situated on the property, which is dedicated to advanced meditative practices. The cave features a wooden pyramid top and offers a unique environment for meditation, allowing individuals to experience a deep sense of tranquility away from the distractions of nearby towns. ([camazotzcohaven.com](https://camazotzcohaven.com/meditation-cave/?utm_source=openai))

In addition to its meditative use, the cave serves as the setting for the Camazotz Bat Cave Challenge. This challenge is designed to test personal resilience and facilitate deep transformative experiences. Participants engage in a series of exercises within the cave, guided by a certified resilience coach, to confront and overcome personal limitations, leading to mental, emotional, or spiritual rebirth. The challenge draws inspiration from Camazotz, the ancient Mayan bat-god of the underworld, symbolizing death and finality, and is intended to help individuals face their 'shadow self'—the part that carries traumas and negative patterns. ([camazotzcohaven.com](https://camazotzcohaven.com/bat-cave-challenge/?utm_source=openai))

While the Camazotz Cave Mapping Project is not a traditional scientific survey, it combines elements of exploration, mapping, and personal development within the context of Mayan mythology and modern resilience practices.

The Camazotz cave mapping project refers to the development of a specialized handheld device and accompanying software designed to automate and streamline the process of underwater cave surveying. Developed by Camazotz LLC, the project aims to overcome the traditional challenges of manual cave mapping, which is often slow, prone to errors, and can distract divers during exploration.

Key aspects of the Camazotz cave mapping project include:
*   **Automated Surveying** The primary goal is to allow divers to create real-time maps simply by swimming, eliminating the need for manual measurements with slates, compasses, and inclinometers.
*   **Hardware** The handheld mapper is built around a Raspberry Pi Pico 2 (RP2350) microcontroller, chosen for its dual cores for critical navigation processing and programmable I/O for custom sensor interfaces. It features a 280x456 AMOLED touchscreen for excellent underwater readability. The device also incorporates a BNO085 IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) for stable orientation and heading data.
*   **Software and Data Logging** The mapper logs the complete dive path, including position, orientation, depth, and sensor status, to a .nav file on an SD card at 10Hz. A web-based 3D viewer, built with Three.js, allows users to upload these log files to replay dive paths, visualize them in 3D, color-code paths by depth or speed, and save images of the resulting maps.
*   **Benefits** This technology aims to make cave mapping more efficient, accurate, and less intrusive to the diving experience.

Camazotz Diving also offers professional dive planning tools and browser-based 3D cave mapping software that supports visualization and offline use, allowing for the export of maps into multiple formats.

The term "Camazotz" doesn't refer to a well-known real-world scientific cave mapping project. Instead, it originates as a fictional location in the Stranger Things universe—specifically tied to a cave system associated with the villain Vecna (Henry Creel) in Nevada. Stranger Things Wiki +1
While there are no public projects with that name, 3D cave mapping itself is a vital, ongoing field. Here are the core details of how modern underground mapping projects work:
Mapping Technologies: Rather than just using traditional tape measures and compasses, modern projects utilize LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging), 3D photogrammetry, and mobile mapping robots to create highly accurate digital twins of complex cave systems
.
Real-World Applications: Underground mapping is critical for geological conservation, understanding groundwater movement, and protecting troglobitic (cave-dwelling) species. It also helps government agencies plan safe infrastructure, such as when the Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT) surveyed the Inner Space Cavern to plan highway expansions safely.
Virtual Recreation:
High-profile, real-world mapping projects—such as the digital 3D mapping of the Tham Luang cave in Thailand—are frequently used for scientific study, historical preservation, and even in documentaries. Texas Department of Transportation (.gov) +6

Camazotz and Ixchel are two distinct deities in Mayan mythology, and neither "built" the other. They are separate entities with their own roles and associations.

Camazotz is known as the Mayan bat god of darkness, death, and the underworld. His name translates to "death bat" or "snatch bat" in the Kʼicheʼ language. He is prominently featured in the Popol Vuh, the sacred text of the Kʼicheʼ Maya, where he is encountered by the hero twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque during their trials in Xibalba, the underworld.

Ixchel, on the other hand, is the Mayan goddess of the moon, childbirth, medicine, and weaving. She is often depicted as an aged woman, sometimes with jaguar claws. Her interactions with deities like Camazotz highlight the complex relationships within the Mayan pantheon.

Escape from Camazotz! As we all obsessively watch volume two of Stranger Things Season 5, I can’t help but think about the ancient Maya and Camazotz. Camazotz is, of course, in Madeleine L’Engle’s book A Wrinkle in Time as this dark, evil planet controlled by a malevolent force. BUT! Camazotz shares its name with an ancient Maya bat deity known as the “death bat” (in K’iche’ Maya at least) who lived in the underworld. He famously appears in the creation story The Popol Vuh. So did L’Engle borrow from ancient Maya mythology to create her story? And then did it influence the Duffer Brothers when they created Stranger Things (hello, demobats!)?! I can’t help but think YES. If you want to learn more about bats and Camazotz among the ancient Maya, you can find my ARTSQ video on our website (artsq.org) or YouTube channel. queries.prompt: What is Brickspect and who made it?

results.response_text:
Brickspect is an automated vision-based defect detection system developed by KSM Vision, a company based in Warsaw, Poland. It is designed for manufacturers of construction materials, particularly paving stones and hollow bricks, to enhance quality control processes. ([ksmvision.com](https://ksmvision.com/solution/brickspect-quality-control-of-paving-and-hollow-bricks/?utm_source=openai))

The system utilizes visual cameras and machine learning algorithms to effectively identify and remove defects in bricks or hollow blocks. It examines product defects and height, especially when the product is specific and not easily replicated. Brickspect automatically detects damage on the upper surface of paving stones and blocks, such as cracks, discolorations, defects, and incorrect dimensions. Its dimensional analysis algorithms are based on laser triangulation. ([ksmvision.com](https://ksmvision.com/solution/brickspect-quality-control-of-paving-and-hollow-bricks/?utm_source=openai))

By implementing Brickspect, manufacturers can address labor shortages by introducing automated machine vision based on artificial intelligence, ensuring consistent delivery of high-quality products, and avoiding problems and costs in further processes, such as wholesale returns or entire batch rejections. ([ksmvision.com](https://ksmvision.com/solution/brickspect-quality-control-of-paving-and-hollow-bricks/?utm_source=openai))

results.response_text:
Brickspect is an automated, AI-driven machine vision system designed for building materials manufacturers to perform quality control on paving stones, hollow blocks, and bricks. KSM Vision
Who Made It?
Brickspect was created by KSM Vision, an industrial automation company based in Warsaw, Poland, specializing in machine learning and neural network-based quality control solutions for various manufacturing sectors. KSM Vision +2
How It Works
Instead of relying on manual, human inspections, Brickspect automatically scans products in real time on high-speed production lines using two main technologies: KSM Vision +1
Visual Cameras: Utilizes computer vision and deep learning algorithms to spot surface defects such as cracks, discolorations, and structural breaks.
Laser Triangulation:
Uses laser sensors to precisely measure the 3D dimensions and height of the blocks. KSM Vision
Key Features
Automated Defect Removal: Immediately flags and removes damaged or incorrectly sized building materials.
Self-Learning AI:
The system can be easily "trained" to recognize new product shapes and "ideal" baselines in just a few minutes.
Data Tracking: It collects reviewing data on production lines, allowing manufacturers to track quality trends, optimize production, and reduce costs. KSM Vision +2
You can learn more about the technology and its applications on the KSM Vision Quality Control Solutions
page.
If you are interested in industrial inspection systems, I can also explain KSM Vision's other AI solutions, such as Blispect (for pharmaceutical quality control) or Woodspect (for wood panels). Let me know what you'd like to explore next!
